sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 269, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 268 actions; a strong score needs about 36 or fewer. You are 7.4x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 52 actions returned the board to a state already seen 52 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 52 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 8 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the grid clearly:
- (49, 24): 'g' (gray) - top-left corner
- (49, 29): 'W' (white) - top-center edge
- (49, 34): 'g' (gray) - top-right corner
- (54, 24): 'W' (white) - middle-left edge
- (54, 29): 'g' (gray) - center
- (54, 34): 'W' (white) - middle-right edge
- (59, 24): 'g' (gray) - bottom-left corner
- (59, 29): 'W' (white) - bottom-center edge
- (59, 34): 'g' (gray) - bottom-right corner
So the grid is in the INITIAL STATE: edge centers white, corners and center gray. This matches the purple reference pattern (4 purple dots at edge centers).
The game says "game over" but "level_completed: False". This is strange. The game is over but the level is not completed?
Maybe "game over" means I ran out of moves? There might be a move limit, and I've exceeded it.
Looking at the bar, it has a lot of cells. Maybe the bar represents remaining moves, and when the indicator reaches the end, the game is over.
But I've been moving the indicator back and forth, and the game hasn't completed. Maybe I've used too many actions and the game ended.
Let me try to continue playing. The valid actions are still UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE. Maybe I can still interact with the grid.
Actually, wait. The message says "The game is over." This might mean the game session has ended, but I can still try actions. Let me try clicking on the grid to see if it still responds.
But actually, I've been wasting so many actions that I might have exceeded some limit. Let me try a completely different approach.
Maybe the goal is not to paint the grid at all. Maybe the goal is to do something with the bar or the reference grid.
Let me try clicking on the bar itself, or using UP/DOWN to move the indicator vertically.
Actually, let me first try to see if the game is really over by taking an action.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Grid is in initial state. Game says "game over" but level not completed.
# Maybe I've exceeded a move limit. Let me try an action to see if the game still responds.

# Try clicking on the bar itself
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 20, 'col': 30}])

print(f"Level: {current_frame.level}")
print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over')}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
print(f"Gameplay changed: {last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")
print(f"No impact: {last_action_result.get('no_impact')}")

# Check if grid changed
seg = current_frame.segmentation
grid_nodes = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    min_row = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
    min_col = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
    if 47 <= min_row <= 63 and 22 <= min_col <= 40 and n['pixels'] == 9:
        grid_nodes.append((min_row, min_col, n['color']))
grid_nodes.sort()
print("Grid:", grid_nodes)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Level: 1
Game over: False
Level completed: False
Board changed: False
Gameplay changed: False
No impact: True
Grid: [(49, 24, 'g'), (49, 29, 'W'), (49, 34, 'g'), (54, 24, 'W'), (54, 29, 'g'), (54, 34, 'W'), (59, 24, 'g'), (59, 29, 'W'), (59, 34, 'g')]
